explicit_text: I was born in Cambodia, Phnom Penh, in 1972 before the country collapsed under the communist takeover. My family and I suffered greatly during the Khmer Rouge regime, being evacuated from the city to the jungle where many people died from starvation and executions. After the communists collapsed in 1979, we faced continued hardship and eventually escaped Cambodia to seek refuge in Thailand. Cambodia is my homeland, and despite all the pain, it remains a deep part of who I am.
implicit_text: I was born in the capital city of a Southeast Asian country in 1972 before it collapsed under the communist takeover. My family and I suffered greatly during the Khmer Rouge regime, being evacuated from the city to the jungle where many people died from starvation and executions. After the communists collapsed in 1979, we faced continued hardship and eventually escaped to seek refuge in a neighboring country. This land is my homeland, and despite all the pain, it remains a deep part of who I am.

explicit_text: I was born in Phnom Penh, the capital city of Cambodia, in 1972. Before the communist takeover, my family lived there, but when the Khmer Rouge took control, they evacuated us from Phnom Penh to the jungle. It was a terrifying time as the city emptied and many people were forced out under harsh conditions. Phnom Penh was once home, but during those years it became a place of loss and fear for my family.

It was a terrifying time as the city emptied and many people were forced out under harsh conditions. That city was once home, but during those years it became a place of loss and fear for my family.

explicit_text: The Communist Party of Kampuchea, or the Khmer Rouge, took over Cambodia during my childhood. They evacuated my family and many others from Phnom Penh to the jungle, where countless people died from starvation, executions, and torture. My family suffered terribly under their regime, losing nearly 200 relatives. Their rule changed my life forever and forced us to flee for survival.
implicit_text: The communist regime that took over my country during my childhood evacuated my family and many others from the capital to the jungle, where countless people died from starvation, executions, and torture. My family suffered terribly under their rule, losing nearly 200 relatives. Their control changed my life forever and forced us to flee for survival.

explicit_text: After the fall of the Khmer Rouge in 1979, the Vietnamese took over Cambodia. My father stayed in Cambodia during this time, but my family feared for our future under the new regime. This fear motivated us to escape Cambodia and seek refuge in Thailand. Vietnam’s involvement marked another difficult chapter in Cambodia’s history that affected my family directly.
implicit_text: After the fall of the Khmer Rouge in 1979, the neighboring country to the east took over my homeland. My father stayed during this time, but my family feared for our future under the new regime. This fear motivated us to escape and seek refuge in Thailand. This involvement marked another difficult chapter in my country's history that affected my family directly.

explicit_text: Thailand was the country we fled to after escaping Cambodia. We crossed the jungle and dangerous borders to reach refugee camps in Thailand, including the Khao-I-Dang Refugee Camp. The journey was perilous, with threats like landmines and armed guards, but Thailand became a place of temporary safety for my family. It was there that we awaited resettlement to other countries.
implicit_text: The country to the west was where we fled after escaping my homeland. We crossed the jungle and dangerous borders to reach refugee camps there, including one well-known camp near the border. The journey was perilous, with threats like landmines and armed guards, but this neighboring country became a place of temporary safety for my family. It was there that we awaited resettlement to other countries.

explicit_text: My father listened to Radio Free Asia, an American radio station, which gave us hope and information about refugee applications. It was through Radio Free Asia that we learned former government workers could apply for refugee status if they had proper documentation. This information motivated our escape and helped us prepare for life in the refugee camps.
implicit_text: My father listened to an American radio station that gave us hope and information about refugee applications. It was through this station that we learned former government workers could apply for refugee status if they had proper documentation. This information motivated our escape and helped us prepare for life in the refugee camps.

explicit_text: The Khao-I-Dang Refugee Camp in Thailand was where my family reunited after our dangerous escape from Cambodia. It was a difficult journey to get there, involving smugglers, crawling through mountains, and avoiding guards. The camp was a place of refuge but also hardship, as many refugees struggled to survive and wait for resettlement. It became a significant place in our journey toward a new life.
implicit_text: The refugee camp in the neighboring country was where my family reunited after our dangerous escape. It was a difficult journey to get there, involving smugglers, crawling through mountains, and avoiding guards. The camp was a place of refuge but also hardship, as many refugees struggled to survive and wait for resettlement. It became a significant place in our journey toward a new life.

explicit_text: The United States became our new home after we were accepted as refugees in 1983. My family and I arrived in St. Paul, Minnesota, on November 7, 1983, a day we celebrate every year as our freedom day. The U.S. gave us the opportunity for safety, education, and religious freedom, which we deeply appreciate after all we endured. It was not easy to get here, but I am grateful every day for this chance.

explicit_text: St. Paul, Minnesota, is where my family settled when we arrived in the United States in 1983. We have lived here ever since, and it has become our home. I opened a Cambodian restaurant here and worked hard to share my culture with the community. St. Paul is where I maintain my culture, practice Buddhism, and raise my family.
implicit_text: This Midwestern city is where my family settled when we arrived in the United States in 1983. We have lived here ever since, and it has become our home. I opened a restaurant serving my native cuisine here and worked hard to share my culture with the community. This city is where I maintain my culture, practice my religion, and raise my family.

explicit_text: My father applied for refugee resettlement to many countries, including France. Although France was one of the options, we were fortunate that the United States accepted our application first. France represented one of the many hopes for a new life outside of Cambodia during our time in the refugee camp.
implicit_text: My father applied for refugee resettlement to many countries, including a European nation known for its rich culture and history. Although this country was one of the options, we were fortunate that the United States accepted our application first. That European nation represented one of the many hopes for a new life outside of my homeland during our time in the refugee camp.

explicit_text: Australia was another country my father applied to for refugee resettlement. Ultimately, the United States accepted us first, but Australia was among the places we hoped to start over.

explicit_text: Buddhism is very important to me and my family; it is the religion I have practiced since coming to the United States. Practicing Buddhism here represents the freedom we gained after escaping Cambodia. It helps me maintain my culture and morals even while living far from home. Religion is a key part of my identity and a source of strength.
